# @zakkster/lite-defer

> Deferred reactive values for @zakkster/lite-signal: defer() returns a value
> that lags its source and catches up on a scheduler, coalescing a synchronous
> burst into one downstream update -- a concurrency frontier with a pending()
> staleness signal.

A frontier in the reactive graph. Urgent consumers read the source and see every
change immediately; expensive consumers read the deferred value and update on the
scheduler's cadence (a frame, an idle slot, a microtask) instead of on every set.
This is useDeferredValue / Solid's createDeferred for lite-signal.

Peer dependency: @zakkster/lite-signal ^1.5.0 (uses createRoot). ESM only.
DOM-free core. MIT.

## Model

Model A (useDeferredValue): the source is computed EAGERLY; what the frontier
defers is the WORK DOWNSTREAM of the deferred value. To defer an expensive
DERIVATION, defer its cheap input and derive from the deferred value:

    const q = defer(rawQuery);                     // cheap signal, deferred
    const results = computed(() => search(q()));   // search runs on q's cadence

## Schedulers -- (flush: () => void) => void

- microtask   coalesce to end of current task. DEFAULT. No host APIs.
- raf         coalesce to next animation frame (frame cadence). Falls back ~16ms.
- idle        coalesce to an idle slot (lowest priority). Falls back ~1ms.
- timeout(ms) factory: coalesce to setTimeout(ms) (a debounce cadence).
- any (flush) => void works, including lite-raf's frame scheduler.

Coalescing is built in: defer schedules at most one flush per burst.

## Entry points

- createDeferrer(reg) -> { defer, deferEffect }   // bind to any registry >=1.5.0
- defer(source, opts?)                            // default registry
- deferEffect(fn, opts?)                          // default registry

## defer(source, { schedule?, equals? }) -> Deferred

- source: an accessor () => T (a signal satisfies it).
- Seeds SYNCHRONOUSLY to the source's current value. Lags on subsequent change;
  catches up to the LATEST value when the scheduler fires (burst coalesced).
- equals: gates the catch-up (default Object.is). schedule: default microtask.
- Returns a callable accessor:
  - d()          the current deferred (lagging) value.
  - d.pending()  () => boolean. TRUE the instant the source moves ahead (synchronous),
                 FALSE again when the deferred value catches up. The frontier
                 indicator: dim stale content / show a spinner.
  - d.dispose()  stop tracking; dispose the deferred + staleness signals.

## deferEffect(fn, { schedule? }) -> dispose

- An effect whose runs are coalesced onto a scheduler (at most one run per burst,
  e.g. once per frame). The INITIAL run is also scheduled (engine semantics).
- Thin wrapper over the engine's effect(fn, { scheduler }); provided so the named
  schedulers have a home and the pattern reads clearly.

## Ownership

defer's internals are detached (createRoot), so an enclosing scope re-running
never tears the deferred value down. The caller owns teardown via d.dispose().
Call defer once at setup.

## Zero-GC

The deferred signal, the staleness signal, the tracking effect, and the flush
thunk are fixed per defer. A churn of source-set + catch-up allocates nothing on
the engine pool (20k cycles flat): the tracking effect re-tracks on a stable read
order (cursor reuse), both signal sets are allocation-free.
Honest non-claim: the scheduler's own queue entry (a microtask, a rAF callback,
a timer) is the host's, not the engine pool's.

## Gotchas

- Model A: defer does NOT defer the source's own computation. Reading the source
  inside the tracking effect forces it synchronously; to defer an expensive
  computation, defer its cheap input upstream.
- deferEffect's first run is scheduled, not synchronous.
- pending() flips to true synchronously on a source change even though the value
  catch-up is deferred -- that is the point (instant staleness, lazy value).
